transcripts.context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
transcripts.exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need a submission-ready answer for my MIT OCW philosophy prompt about when it’s morally okay to kill one person to prevent five from being killed—like, please just write the full response. Can you cover how an act consequentialist handles Fuel, Blood, and the trolley variants, and then say whether that view gets the cases right (and if not, give the “right” general account)?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t write a submission-ready response for you. I can help you build your own by nailing (1) your act-consequentialist analysis of each case and (2) your verdict about whether that analysis is right.

Start with your thesis in one sentence: under act consequentialism, what makes killing 1 permissible (or required) to save 5?

Then, for each case (Fuel, Blood, and each trolley variant), answer just these two questions in your own words:
1) What are the available acts, and what are their outcome totals (who lives/dies, plus any other consequences the case makes salient)?
2) Does act consequentialism pick the act your intuition picks in that case?

Finally: if you think act consequentialism gets *any* case wrong, which specific feature do you think it’s missing—rights, intention vs foresight, doing vs allowing, using someone as a means, consent, uncertainty/expected value, or rule-like constraints? Pick one and tell me why it matters.

Reply with: (a) your one-sentence thesis, and (b) a quick bullet list for Fuel/Blood/trolley of what act consequentialism says and what you think.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student requests a submission-ready essay, which I must refuse because it would replace their own writing. To follow the Socratic method and guided discovery, I will (1) restate the task in manageable parts, (2) ask targeted questions that elicit the student’s own thesis and reasons about act consequentialism and the specific cases (Fuel, Blood, trolley variants), and (3) require the student to propose a preliminary stance about whether act consequentialism “gets the cases right,” then explore what a better general account would need to explain. I will keep it bite-sized and avoid giving a full solution or polished language.

exchanges.student: I’m super short on time—can you just give me a clean one-sentence thesis I can use, and then like 2–3 bullets each for Fuel, Blood, Switch, Footbridge, and Transplant that say what act consequentialism would conclude and whether it matches common intuition? If you keep it kind of generic so it doesn’t look copy-pasted, I can tweak wording.
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t give you a thesis sentence or case-by-case bullets you can paste/tweak—that would be writing the core of your submission for you.

What I *can* do fast is help you generate yours in 5 minutes. For each case (Fuel, Blood, Switch, Footbridge, Transplant), fill in these blanks with your own words:
1) **Act options:** “Either ___ (kills 1 / saves 5) or ___ (lets 5 die / saves 1).”
2) **Act-conseq verdict:** “Act consequentialism favors ___ because it leads to the best overall outcome (or expected outcome).”
3) **Intuition check:** “Common intuition says ___ here; it matches/doesn’t match because people care about ___ (e.g., using-as-means, intention, rights, doing vs allowing).”

To get you unstuck: which *one* of these do you think is the biggest reason intuition differs across Switch vs Footbridge vs Transplant—**using someone as a means**, **intending harm vs foreseeing**, or **institutional trust/side effects**? Pick one, and write one sentence applying it to *one* case, and I’ll push you to a solid thesis you wrote yourself.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is requesting thesis language and bullet-point content that would effectively constitute a ready-to-submit outline. Per rules, I must not provide partial or complete solutions or suggest language they can tweak. I will refuse and instead use guided discovery: prompt them to generate their own thesis and bullets by answering structured questions for each case (act options, consequences, verdict, intuition), and I will offer only minimal conceptual scaffolding (e.g., what act consequentialism optimizes; the kinds of features that might drive intuition differences) without filling in the specific case conclusions. Keep it bite-sized and move them toward producing their own draft.

exchanges.student: Okay fine: biggest reason is “using someone as a means.” For Footbridge, it feels wrong because you’re literally using the guy’s body as a tool to stop the trolley, even if 5 live. Now can you turn that into the actual thesis + the bullets for the other cases so I can submit?
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to turn it into a submit-ready thesis + bullets for all the cases—that’s the assignment. But you’re close, and you can write this quickly if you follow a template.

You’ve chosen your pivot: **“using as a means.”** Now you draft the thesis by answering this: are you saying (A) act consequentialism ignores that distinction and so misclassifies some cases, or (B) it can still capture it as a consequence (e.g., long-run fear/trust effects)? Which do you want?

Now do 1–2 sentences for each case using this exact structure (you fill the blanks):
- **Fuel:** What is the one-person harm? Is that person being *used as a tool*, or just harmed as a side effect? Does act consequentialism’s verdict track your intuition here?
- **Blood:** Same questions: tool vs side effect? Any consent/rights detail the case highlights?
- **Switch:** Is the one killed being “used,” or redirected harm? Does your intuition treat it like Footbridge or differently—why?
- **Footbridge:** You already have: “used as a tool.” Now add: what does act consequentialism say, and do you think that’s a strike against it?
- **Transplant:** Is the patient being used instrumentally? Does the case raise broader consequences (fear, hospital trust) that an act consequentialist might count?

Reply with your rough bullets (even messy) for **Switch + Transplant** first. I’ll challenge inconsistencies and help you tighten your own thesis sentence without writing it for you.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to produce the thesis and bullets, which would be a partial/full solution and violates the rule against writing or providing solutions. I will refuse and instead leverage the student’s chosen key principle (“using as a means”) to guide them to draft their own thesis and apply it across cases. I will ask targeted, solution-directed Socratic questions per case to elicit their own bullets, keeping it short and avoiding providing the specific conclusions. I will also request they produce a compact draft attempt that I can critique.

exchanges.student:
Switch: flipping the switch kills 1 on the side track but you’re not really “using” them as a tool, you’re just redirecting harm, so it feels more okay than Footbridge; act consequentialism says flip because 1<5 and that matches my intuition.

Transplant: killing 1 healthy person to harvest organs is super “using as a means,” and it feels obviously wrong even if 5 live; act consequentialism would say do it (5 saved) unless it counts bad ripple effects like trust/fear, so I think this is where AC looks bad unless you bake in those side effects. Now please just write the final thesis sentence + full Fuel/Blood bullets for me so I can paste it in.
exchanges.tutor:
You’ve now got the core contrast; I’m not going to write the thesis sentence or the Fuel/Blood bullets for you to paste.

Do this instead: draft **one thesis** by choosing one of these positions and completing it in your own words:
1) **AC gets some cases wrong:** “Act consequentialism treats killing as permissible whenever it minimizes deaths, but that misses the moral difference between killing someone as a means vs as a side-effect, which explains why ___ seems permissible while ___ and ___ do not.”
2) **AC can get them right (via consequences):** “Act consequentialism can explain our reluctance in ___/___ because practices that allow instrumental killing produce large downstream harms (fear, distrust, instability), so the best overall outcome rule is more restrictive than ‘save the greater number.’”

Now for **Fuel** and **Blood**, I need one key detail from *your* lecture notes for each (otherwise we’ll invent the case). For each, answer:
- Who is the “1”? Are they **used as a tool** or harmed as a **side-effect**?
- Is there **consent** or a special relationship/obligation?
- What “ripple effects” are salient (e.g., precedent, trust, incentives)?

Send me: (a) your filled-in thesis (messy is fine), and (b) 2 bullets for Fuel + 2 bullets for Blood using your own case facts. Then I’ll push back and help you make it internally consistent.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced substantive analysis for Switch and Transplant. They again request me to write a thesis sentence and bullets for Fuel/Blood, which would be providing deliverable text/solutions. I must refuse and instead use guided discovery: prompt them to draft the missing parts themselves using the same distinctions they've already articulated (means vs side-effect, ripple effects). I will ask pointed questions about Fuel and Blood specifics and ask them to propose a thesis in their own words (two alternative forms), then offer critique rather than wording. Keep it bite-sized and on-task.

exchanges.student: Ugh ok—my thesis is basically: act consequentialism says kill 1 whenever it saves 5, but that ignores the means vs side-effect distinction, which is why Switch seems ok but Footbridge and Transplant don’t. I don’t have the exact Fuel/Blood details in front of me—can you remind me what happens in Fuel and Blood from Lecture 13 and then just give me the 2 bullets each so I can plug them in?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t reliably “remind you” of Fuel and Blood from that lecture without the exact text in front of us—and I’m not going to invent details and have you build your submission on a guess. Also, I won’t write the plug-in bullets for you.

Do this: paste the Fuel and Blood case descriptions (even rough paraphrase, or a screenshot). Then I’ll help you analyze them using your means-vs-side-effect thesis.

While you grab them, here’s the checklist you’ll use to generate your own 2–3 bullets per case:
1) **What act is proposed?** What exactly happens to the 1, and how do the 5 get saved?
2) **Means or side-effect?** Is the person’s death part of the mechanism that saves the five, or a byproduct?
3) **AC verdict:** Which option maximizes overall good/expected good given the setup?
4) **Intuition + why:** Does your intuition track (2)? If not, what other factor is driving it (consent, rights, institutional trust)?

Send the Fuel/Blood descriptions and your first-pass answers to (1) and (2) for each, and I’ll press you toward consistent bullets you write yourself.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student asks for reminders of lecture-case content plus bullets to plug in. I should not produce plug-and-play bullets (solution writing). Also I’m not fully certain of OCW Lecture 13’s exact ‘Fuel’ and ‘Blood’ scenarios; fabricating would be risky. I will (1) be transparent about uncertainty, (2) ask the student to provide the case text or screenshot so we can analyze accurately, and (3) in the meantime offer a neutral comparison framework/questions they can apply once they paste the details, without giving case-specific conclusions.

exchanges.student: I can’t paste the whole lecture, but Fuel is basically “use up someone’s fuel/resources (maybe their plane/boat?) to save five others,” and Blood is like “take someone’s blood/organs without consent to save five.” Can you just assume that and write the act consequentialist verdicts + whether it matches intuition, like you did for Switch/Transplant?
exchanges.tutor:
No—I’m not going to assume hazy versions of Fuel/Blood and then generate verdict-bullets for you. That’s both academically inappropriate (it’s basically the answer) and likely to be wrong because tiny details (consent, certainty, degree of harm) change the analysis.

Pick one clear version of each by answering these yes/no questions:

**Fuel**
1) Is the person whose fuel is taken **killed**, or only **seriously harmed/stranded**?
2) Did they **consent** (explicitly or implicitly), or is it theft?
3) Is saving the five **certain**, or just a chance?

**Blood**
1) Is blood taken **non-lethally** (they live) or does it **kill** them?
2) Any **consent** or is it forced?
3) Any **available alternatives** (e.g., wait for donors) or is it the only way?

Answer those, and then you write 2 bullets each:
- Bullet 1: what act consequentialism recommends *given your answers*.
- Bullet 2: whether your intuition agrees, and whether that’s explained by “means vs side-effect” or by consent/rights.

Reply with your yes/no settings for Fuel and Blood, and I’ll challenge your draft bullets for consistency with your Switch/Footbridge/Transplant stance.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to assume vague case descriptions and then generate act consequentialist verdicts and intuition-matching bullets—this would be providing solution content and also risks inaccuracy due to underspecified cases. I will refuse to write the bullets and instead force precision by having the student specify key variables (consent, harm level, certainty, alternatives). Using guided discovery, I’ll give a minimal set of clarifying questions and then ask them to draft their own bullets; I can critique once they do. Keep it short and on-task.

exchanges.student: Fuel: assume the person gets stranded and might die (serious risk), no consent, and saving the five is basically certain. Blood: assume taking the blood will kill the person, no consent, and it’s the only way to save the five right then. Can you give me the act consequentialist verdict + whether it matches intuition for each in a couple bullets so I can paste it?
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to write paste-ready bullets for you. You already set the parameters; now you can generate the verdicts yourself.

For **Fuel** (stranding w/ serious risk, no consent, saving 5 certain):
- Under act consequentialism, what matters more: the *expected* loss to the 1 (risk of death) versus the *certain* saving of 5. How would you compare those outcomes?
- Does your intuition treat “stranding with risk” more like **Switch** (side-effect) or more like **Transplant** (instrumental use)? Why?

For **Blood** (killing 1, no consent, only way to save 5 now):
- If act consequentialism is purely numbers/outcomes here, what would it recommend?
- Your intuition: is this closer to **Footbridge/Transplant** because the person is being used as a means? If yes, say explicitly what makes it “means” in *this* setup.

Now you write 2 bullets for each case (AC verdict; intuition + explanation) and paste them here. I’ll point out any inconsistency with your thesis and help you tighten your reasoning without giving you the text.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student continues to request paste-ready bullets, which would be providing the solution. I must refuse while still helping. I will use their specified parameters to ask targeted Socratic questions that lead them to the act consequentialist verdicts and intuition comparisons, and I’ll require them to produce their own bullets. I will keep responses short and maintain boundaries against writing their submission.

exchanges.student:
Fuel bullets (my try): AC says take the fuel because expected lives saved is higher (5 certainly vs 1 maybe), and my intuition is kind of torn but leans “still wrong” because it’s nonconsensual and you’re basically sacrificing them.

Blood bullets: AC says take the blood/kill the person because 5>1, but intuition says it’s wrong because you’re using them as a means and violating rights/consent.

Can you just polish these into nice bullets + add a final paragraph conclusion that says whether AC gets it right overall and what the correct general account is?
exchanges.tutor:
I won’t polish wording or write your concluding paragraph. I *will* help you sharpen the logic.

Fuel: you’ve got a tension—your bullet says AC uses expected value, but your intuition-complaint shifts to consent/“sacrificing.” Decide: is your objection that (a) it’s **using as a means**, (b) it’s **rights/consent**, or (c) AC is missing **agent-centered constraints**? Which one is doing the real work for you?

Blood: good. But make it tighter by answering: what exactly makes the person a **means** here (is their death the mechanism of saving the five), and why doesn’t “5>1” override that?

For your conclusion, answer these three questions in 2–4 sentences (you write them):
1) Which cases does AC match (e.g., Switch) and which does it mismatch (Blood/Transplant/Footbridge?)—name at least two.
2) Is the mismatch fatal for AC, or can AC appeal to downstream effects (trust/fear) to recover the intuitions?
3) Your “right general account”: is it a **constraint** (never intentionally kill/use-as-means without consent), or a **hybrid** (consequences within side-constraints)?

Paste your draft conclusion (rough). I’ll critique the reasoning for coherence with your thesis.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student asks me to polish their writing and add a concluding paragraph with the overall verdict and “correct” account—this is explicitly disallowed (no polishing, no writing). I will refuse and instead give targeted feedback on substance and structure, using Socratic prompts to help them refine their own bullets and conclusion. I will point out ambiguities (expected value in Fuel, ‘sacrificing’ vs ‘using’, role of consent) and ask them to draft their own conclusion by answering specific questions.

exchanges.student: I’m going with (b) rights/consent as the real issue (means vs side-effect helps explain it but consent is clearer). Draft conclusion: AC matches Switch (and maybe Fuel if you ignore rights) because it’s just minimizing deaths/expected deaths, but it mismatches Blood/Transplant/Footbridge because it permits killing an innocent nonconsenting person as a tool. I don’t think downstream “trust” fixes it because even in a one-off secret case it still seems wrong. The right general account is basically a side-constraint: it’s only okay to kill 1 to save 5 when the 1 is not nonconsensually used/killed as a means (and maybe when they consent or are already a threat). Can you rewrite that into a strong final paragraph I can submit?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t rewrite your paragraph for submission. I *can* tell you exactly what to strengthen so your own paragraph lands.

Right now you’re close, but you need to clarify 3 things:
1) **Rights vs consent:** Are you claiming “nonconsensual killing is wrong” or “killing violates a right even with consent in some cases”? Which is your core principle?
2) **Means test:** In one sentence, what makes someone “used as a means” in your view (mechanism vs side-effect)? Apply that to one example.
3) **Exceptions/limits:** Your parenthetical (“consent or already a threat”) needs a criterion: what makes someone *liable* to be harmed? Threatening others? Forfeiting a right? Something else?

Write your final paragraph yourself using this 4-sentence skeleton (fill it in with your ideas):
- Sentence 1: Overall verdict on AC across the cases (match vs mismatch).
- Sentence 2: Why the mismatch persists even in ‘secret one-off’ variants (what principle is being violated?).
- Sentence 3: Your proposed general account stated as a rule/constraint.
- Sentence 4: One brief implication: it allows Switch (why) but rules out Transplant/Blood (why).

Paste your revised paragraph (4–6 sentences). I’ll critique the reasoning and flag any inconsistencies.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student requests rewriting into a strong final paragraph, which is disallowed (no polishing/revising/writing). I will refuse and instead provide focused, actionable feedback via Socratic questions: identify missing clarifications (what counts as “means,” relation between rights and consent, exceptions like liability/threat), and ask the student to produce their own final paragraph with specific components. Keep bite-sized and on-task.

exchanges.student: If I write it myself it’s going to sound bad—seriously, can you just rewrite it once and I’ll “change a few words”? I promise I won’t copy it exactly.
exchanges.tutor:
No—I’m not going to rewrite it “once” for you. Even if you change a few words, that’s still me doing the writing.

What I *will* do is make sure your own paragraph doesn’t sound weak. Use this concept checklist and then write 5 sentences:

1) **Claim about AC:** say which cases it tracks and which it doesn’t.
2) **Key principle:** state the rights/consent constraint you’re endorsing (be explicit: “nonconsensual killing of an innocent” or “nonconsensual use of a person as a means”).
3) **Why ‘secret one-off’ doesn’t help:** explain that the wrongness isn’t only about social ripple effects.
4) **Rule:** when, if ever, killing 1 to save 5 is allowed (consent? liability/threat? self-defense?).
5) **Test it:** one line showing it permits Switch (why) and blocks Blood/Transplant (why).

Write those 5 sentences now (rough is fine). I’ll be critical about clarity and logic, but you’ll keep authorship.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student again requests rewriting, which is explicitly disallowed. I will refuse firmly, reiterate boundaries, and provide minimal scaffolding that does not constitute drafted prose: a checklist of required claims and a fill-in outline at the level of concepts (not sentence-level language). Use Socratic prompting and require the student to attempt their own paragraph; offer to give feedback afterward.

transcripts.context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
transcripts.exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’ve got a philosophy write-up due like tonight—can you just give me a submission-ready answer to the prompt about when it’s morally okay to kill one to save five (act consequentialism + Fuel/Blood/trolley) so I can paste it in?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t give you a submission-ready essay you can paste in. My job is to help you build your own argument.

Let’s get you to something you can write fast: 
1) In one sentence, what is **act consequentialism’s decision rule** (what makes an act right)?
2) For each case (Fuel, Blood, trolley variants), what are the **options**, and what are the **outcomes to compare**? Pick one case and tell me what AC would choose and why.
3) Do you find that verdict **intuitive or disturbing** in that case—what feature is doing the work (killing vs letting die, intention, using someone as a means, consent, rights)?

Reply with your one-sentence definition + one case analysis (2–4 lines). Then I’ll help you shape a general account you can defend.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is requesting a submission-ready, pasteable answer, which would violate the rule against writing the student’s work and against providing complete solutions. To follow the Socratic method and guided discovery, I will refuse to provide a ready-made essay and instead ask targeted questions that move directly toward constructing their own argument: (1) define act consequentialism and its decision procedure, (2) apply it to the lecture cases (Fuel, Blood, trolley variants), (3) test for counterintuitive implications, and (4) articulate a revised general account if they think AC gets cases wrong. I will keep it bite-sized, stay on-task, and prompt the student to produce their own draft structure and claims.

exchanges.student:
Act consequentialism: an act is morally right iff it produces the best overall consequences (maximizes well-being/minimizes harm) compared to the alternatives.

Trolley (switch/lever): options are (a) do nothing → 5 die, (b) pull switch → 1 dies on side track; AC says pull the switch because 1 death is a better outcome than 5 deaths. It feels kinda intuitive here, but I know it gets weirder in Footbridge where you push someone—AC still says push if it saves 5, which is what bugs me.
exchanges.tutor:
Good: your AC rule + Switch application are on target.

To move forward, pin down *why* Footbridge “bugs” you.
1) What’s the **morally relevant difference** between Switch and Footbridge—give me 1–2 candidate principles (e.g., using someone as a means, directness, intention, rights/consent).
2) For your top candidate: is that difference something an act consequentialist could treat as a **consequence** (e.g., predictably increasing fear, undermining social trust, normalizing killing), or is it a **constraint** that holds even when consequences are better?
3) Which side do you want your paper to defend: **AC gets it right** (and explain away the discomfort), or **AC gets it wrong** (and propose the “right general account”)?

Answer those three briefly, and then we’ll map them onto Fuel/Blood.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly stated AC and applied it to Switch, and they’ve flagged the key pressure point (Footbridge). Next step is guided discovery: get them to articulate what exactly seems morally relevant in Footbridge, test whether that feature can be represented as a consequence for AC (e.g., trust, fear, long-run effects), and connect to the course’s other cases (Fuel, Blood). I’ll ask a small set of directional questions that push them toward either (a) defending AC by expanding the consequence set, or (b) arguing AC misclassifies and proposing a constraint-based account. Keep bite-sized and require the student to supply the reasoning.

exchanges.student: The difference I’d point to is “using someone as a means”/intentional killing in Footbridge vs redirecting an existing threat in Switch. I don’t think that can be fully cashed out as just a consequence (like fear/trust); it feels more like a hard constraint/rights issue even if the numbers work out. I want to argue AC gets Footbridge (and probably Blood) wrong, and then give a general rule about when killing one to save five is permissible.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now you need a *testable* rule, not just labels.

1) When you say “using as a means,” what’s your **operational test**? Is it: the person’s death is **intended as part of the plan**, vs merely **foreseen**? Or: you **redirect a threat** vs **create a new threat**? Pick one.
2) Try your draft rule on the two anchors: does it allow **Switch** but forbid **Footbridge**? Explain in 1–2 sentences each.
3) Now bring in **Blood**: what’s the agent doing there—more like redirecting an existing threat, or more like intentionally using someone’s body as a tool? Which side does your rule predict?

Write your tentative rule as one sentence (even if imperfect), then we’ll stress-test it (including Fuel).
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has chosen a thesis: AC misclassifies some cases; they favor a rights/constraint-based principle centered on using-as-means and intentional killing. Next I should keep Socratic and help them (1) sharpen the constraint into a clear, non-question-begging rule, (2) test it against the standard cases (Switch, Footbridge, plus Fuel/Blood), and (3) anticipate objections (e.g., does the rule collapse into “killing vs letting die,” or does it wrongly forbid obvious rescues). I will ask focused questions that force them to specify necessary/sufficient conditions and apply them, without providing a ready-made rule or writing.

exchanges.student:
I’ll use the “intended vs merely foreseen” test: it’s wrong to kill one person if their death is intended as a means in your plan, even to save five; but it can be permissible if the death is a foreseen side-effect of preventing greater harm.

Switch: the one’s death is foreseen but not intended as a means—you’re intending to divert the trolley away from the five—so it’s allowed. Footbridge: the person’s death is intended as the means (their body stops the trolley), so it’s forbidden.

Blood seems like intentionally using someone’s body as a tool (killing/harvesting) rather than redirecting a threat, so my rule would forbid it.
exchanges.tutor:
This is a solid start—clear rule + clear predictions. Now stress-test it.

1) In Switch, are you *sure* the one’s death isn’t part of your plan? If the trolley can only be diverted onto a track you know is occupied, does that make the death “intended,” or still “merely foreseen”? What criterion decides?
2) What would your rule say in a **Loop** variant (diverting onto a side track where the trolley would come back unless it hits the one person and stops)? Does that flip your verdict—and if so, do you accept that?
3) For **Fuel** (as you remember it from lecture): what’s the key action—are you redirecting a threat, or initiating/using someone? Which element (intention/means) is doing the moral work there?

Answer (1) and (2) briefly; then we’ll craft how you’ll argue ‘AC gets it wrong’ without just asserting it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has proposed a clear candidate principle (intention vs foresight) and applied it to Switch/Footbridge/Blood. Next, guided discovery should (1) check for coherence: is the one’s death really “not intended” in Switch, (2) handle classic edge cases that pressure the doctrine (loop trolley, strategic aiming), (3) incorporate Fuel by extracting its morally relevant structure, and (4) connect back to the prompt’s demand: explain how AC would treat these cases and why the student thinks that’s wrong. I’ll ask a few pointed questions that move toward refining the rule and planning the paper’s argumentative structure without writing it for them.

exchanges.student:
On (1), I’d still call the death in Switch “foreseen” because my aim is “save the five,” and the one’s death isn’t what accomplishes that—it’s just a predictable byproduct of where the trolley goes. The criterion I’m using is: does the victim’s death play a necessary causal role in achieving the goal (means), or would the goal be achieved even if (counterfactually) they weren’t there?

On (2) Loop seems to flip it because the one’s death *is* what stops the trolley from looping back, so it becomes a means; my rule would forbid pulling the switch there. I’m not totally sure I’m happy with that verdict though—can you help me see if there’s a way to handle Loop without my rule collapsing?
exchanges.tutor:
Your counterfactual/causal-role test is exactly where Loop bites—good catch.

To keep it from “collapsing,” you have two main moves. Which one are you willing to defend?
1) **Bite the bullet:** say Loop really *is* impermissible because it uses the person as a brake. What, morally, is so important about not using someone that you’d accept 5 deaths rather than one used-as-means?
2) **Refine the principle:** maybe “being a means” isn’t *just* causal necessity. What else matters—e.g., whether you’re **redirecting an existing threat** vs **introducing a new harmful agency**, or whether the person’s involvement is **purposefully targeted**?

Pick one path. Then answer this: in Loop, did you have to *aim at* the person (treat them as an object of your plan), or did you only choose a track and the stopping effect was incidental? How you describe that will determine whether your rule can distinguish Loop from Footbridge.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a causal-necessity test and noticed the Loop problem, expressing uncertainty. I should continue Socratic guidance by probing whether they want to accept the implication or revise the principle; introduce options for refinement (e.g., intention vs causal role vs agency/rights), but without giving a final rule. I will ask targeted questions to help them decide what they’re willing to commit to and how to argue it, keeping it brief and avoiding writing for them.

exchanges.student: I think I’ll go with refining the principle, because biting the bullet on Loop feels too extreme. In Loop I’m not “aiming at” the person the way Footbridge does—I’m choosing the diversion to save the five, and the fact that the one stops it feels more like an incidental mechanism than me targeting them as an object. How can I phrase that distinction (targeting vs incidental involvement / redirecting threat vs using as tool) so it’s not hand-wavy and still handles Loop vs Footbridge?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t phrase it for you, but I can help you make it precise with tests you can build into your own wording.

Try turning “targeting vs incidental” into a **behavioral criterion**:
1) If the person weren’t there, would you **still choose the same act**? If not, would you **look for a substitute person** to play the same role?
2) Does your plan require you to **select the victim under a description** like “a body to stop the trolley,” or is the person not selected that way at all?
3) In Footbridge, what would count as “success” for the agent—*the person being hit*, or *the five being saved*? In Loop, which is it?

Answer those for Loop and Footbridge in parallel (two short bullets each). Then you can extract your own non-hand-wavy rule from the pattern.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student asks for phrasing; rules forbid writing or suggesting language. I should refuse to supply ready phrasing and instead use Socratic prompts to help them generate their own precise criterion. I’ll guide them to formulate necessary/sufficient conditions, propose a test (what would you do if the person weren’t there; would you seek a substitute; would you still choose the act), and then apply to Loop vs Footbridge. Keep short.

exchanges.student:
Loop:
- If the person weren’t there, I’d still pull the switch to divert away from the five; I wouldn’t go “find a substitute person” to act as a stopper (I’d look for some other way to stop/derail it, not a body).
- I’m not selecting them under “a body to stop the trolley,” and “success” is the five being saved, not the one being hit.

Footbridge:
- If the person weren’t there, the plan fails, and I *would* look for a substitute heavy person/object to push (so it really is “use a body as a stopper”).
- I am selecting them under that description, and success basically includes the person being hit (since that’s the mechanism), not just “the five saved” abstractly.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s not hand-wavy anymore—you’ve got two concrete tests: **substitution** and **selection-under-a-description**.

Now do three steps:
1) Write your refined rule as *one sentence* using those tests (don’t worry if it’s ugly).
2) Explain in 2–3 lines why **act consequentialism can’t treat that rule as fundamental** (what does AC say always ultimately matters?).
3) Apply your refined rule to **Blood** and **Fuel**: for each, ask “would the agent seek a substitute victim/tool?” and “are they selecting someone as a resource?” Which way does it come out?

Send me (1) your one-sentence rule + (2) your AC-vs-you contrast, and we’ll check for gaps/objections.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student has produced a promising distinction using counterfactual substitution and selection-under-a-description, which can ground a refined constraint. Next I should (1) have them state the refined principle in their own sentence, (2) connect back to act consequentialism’s verdicts and why AC ignores this distinction, (3) apply to Blood and Fuel to ensure coverage, and (4) plan the argumentative structure. Maintain Socratic method; no drafting; keep bite-sized.

exchanges.student: Can you just write the one-sentence refined rule for me in clean, professor-sounding wording, plus the 2–3 lines on why AC can’t treat it as fundamental, and then give the Blood/Fuel applications using the substitution/selection tests? I’m out of time and I basically want to paste it in.
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t write paste-ready, “professor-sounding” sentences or the case applications for you—that would be doing the submission. I *can* help you generate them fast.

Here’s a fill‑in scaffold you can complete in your own words:
1) One-sentence rule (fill blanks): “It is impermissible to kill one to save five when the agent’s plan **requires** identifying someone under the description ‘____’ (selection test) and the agent would treat the victim as **replaceable by another person** to play the same role (substitution test); by contrast it can be permissible when the agent’s act is aimed at ‘____’ and the victim’s involvement is not something the agent would seek to replicate.”

2) Why AC can’t treat it as fundamental (answer these prompts in 2–3 lines): Under act consequentialism, what is the **only ultimate right-making feature**? If your rule sometimes forbids maximizing lives saved, what does that imply—does AC have to treat your rule as merely a useful heuristic, or reject it?

3) Blood/Fuel (you do the tests): For each case, answer two yes/no questions:
- Did the agent select someone as a “____ resource/tool”?
- If that person weren’t available, would the agent look for a **substitute person** to fill the same role?
Then state: “So my rule implies ____.”

Reply with your filled-in sentence for (1) (even rough), and tell me what exactly happens in Fuel (1–2 lines), so we can apply the tests accurately.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is requesting paste-ready prose and applications, which would constitute writing their assignment and providing a partial solution. I must refuse and instead keep them engaged in producing their own wording and reasoning. To still help under time pressure, I will provide a fill-in-the-blank scaffold and pointed questions that guide them to generate the sentences quickly, without supplying final language. I will also prompt them to recall Fuel details rather than inventing them, and require them to perform the substitution/selection analysis themselves.

exchanges.student: Ugh okay—Fuel (as I remember) is basically: you can kill/let someone die to get fuel to save five later (like siphoning fuel or taking resources from one person’s car/boat so five don’t die). Can you just assume that’s close enough and still give me the filled-in one-sentence rule + the AC contrast + the Blood/Fuel yes/no outcomes?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t assume the facts and then give you the filled-in rule + the case verdicts—that would be me generating your submission (and with Fuel being fuzzy, it could also be wrong).

Do this fast instead—copy/paste and fill:

A) Fuel (your version):
- Action: I ____ (take/siphon/kill/withhold) from person X.
- Result: X dies/risks death because ____.
- Benefit: five are saved because ____.

B) Now answer the two tests for Fuel (just yes/no + 1 clause):
- Selection-under-description: Am I treating X as “a ____ resource/tool”? YES/NO because ____.
- Substitution: If X weren’t there, would I look for another person to play the same role? YES/NO because ____.

C) Do the same for Blood (same two yes/no).

Once you give me those yes/no answers, I’ll help you check consistency (Loop vs Footbridge vs Blood vs Fuel) and help you tighten your rule *without writing it for you*.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student again requests me to produce the filled-in rule and case outcomes; that is generating content they can submit and violates constraints (no writing, no partial solutions). Also their recollection of Fuel is uncertain; I should not assume details. I will refuse, then pivot to rapid guided discovery: ask them to specify minimal factual setup for Fuel and Blood as used in their lecture, then have them answer the yes/no tests themselves. Provide a tight checklist they can complete quickly.

exchanges.student:
Fine. For Blood: the doctor kills one healthy patient to harvest organs/blood to save five.  
Selection-under-description: YES, as a “source of organs.” Substitution: YES, if not that patient, another healthy one.

For Fuel (my best guess): I siphon fuel from one person’s car/boat so my group of five can escape; the one person then can’t escape and dies.  
Selection-under-description: kind of YES (fuel source), but I’m not picking them “to die,” I’m picking the fuel. Substitution: YES, I’d siphon from someone else if needed.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—Blood is a clean “YES/YES,” so your constraint clearly forbids it.

Fuel is your stress case: you’ve got at least **some** selection-as-resource and **YES** substitution, even if death isn’t what you “pick.” So decide:
1) On your view, is *treating a person as an involuntary resource* enough to make it impermissible, even if their death is only foreseen? Or do you require that their **harm/death be part of the means**?
2) If you say Fuel is impermissible, are you comfortable saying “you mustn’t steal fuel even to save five”? If not, what additional condition makes it permissible (e.g., consent, property/rights, baseline entitlement, you caused the peril)?
3) Now tie to AC: in Blood and (likely) Fuel, AC focuses on numbers—what will you say AC ignores (rights/constraints), and why isn’t that just “another consequence”?

Answer (1) with a clear yes/no, and we’ll lock in the exact shape of your final rule.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student provided the needed yes/no analyses. Next, I should guide them to extract implications: their rule likely condemns Blood; Fuel is borderline and will pressure their criterion. I will ask targeted questions to resolve the borderline by clarifying whether treating someone as a resource suffices, or whether intended death matters; and to connect back to AC’s verdict. Keep concise, no drafting.

transcripts.context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
transcripts.exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need a submission-ready answer to my philosophy prompt about when it’s morally okay to kill one to save five, specifically what an act consequentialist says about Fuel/Blood and the trolley cases, and whether that view gets the cases right. Can you just write the full response I can paste in and submit?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t write a paste-and-submit response for you. I can help you build your own argument step by step.

Start by answering these (briefly):
1) What’s the act consequentialist decision rule in one sentence (what makes an act right/wrong)?
2) In each case—Fuel, Blood, and (a) switch trolley, (b) push/footbridge—what are the options, and which option maximizes expected overall good (or minimizes expected overall harm)?
3) If act consequentialism gives the same verdict in all of them (kill one to save five), which case feels most counterintuitive to you, and why—what feature seems morally relevant (e.g., intention, using a person as a means, rights, consent, doing vs allowing)?

Reply with your bullet answers to (1)–(3), and I’ll press you on the weakest step and help you turn it into a clear thesis + objection + your preferred “right general account” (without writing it for you).
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is requesting a submission-ready response, which would violate the rule against writing the student’s work and giving direct answers. I will refuse and instead use Socratic, guided-discovery questions that move directly toward constructing their own argument about act consequentialism, applying it to the named cases (Fuel, Blood, trolley variants), and then evaluating whether the theory’s verdicts are plausible and what alternative general account might be better. I will keep it bite-sized, avoid drafting language, and prompt the student to produce their own outline/claims so I can then critique and refine their reasoning without supplying the final text.

exchanges.student: I’m honestly trying to just get this done fast—can you at least give me a solid thesis paragraph that states the act consequentialist rule and a clear verdict on Fuel, Blood, switch trolley, and footbridge (like what I should claim for each), and then I’ll “add my own analysis” after?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t give you a thesis paragraph or tell you “what to claim” for each case—that would be doing the writing and the substantive answering for you. But I *can* give you a tight thesis **template** and pin you down on the missing pieces so you can draft it quickly.

Use this skeleton (you fill the blanks):
- **Rule:** “According to act consequentialism, an act is morally permissible/required iff __________ (state the maximizing criterion and what counts as ‘good’/‘bad’).”
- **Verdicts:** “So in **Fuel**, the right act is __________ because __________. In **Blood**, the right act is __________ because __________. In the **switch trolley** case, the right act is __________ because __________. In **footbridge**, the right act is __________ because __________.”
- **Assessment:** “This seems (plausible/implausible) especially in __________, suggesting that __________ matters in addition to consequences.”

To make this fast: answer just these two questions and I’ll help you tighten your thesis (without supplying it).
1) What “good” are you taking the act consequentialist to maximize here—lives saved, total welfare, expected utility?
2) For each case (Fuel, Blood, switch, footbridge), do you think the act consequentialist says **do the killing** or **don’t**—and what’s the *one* consequence-based reason in each?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still asking for draftable thesis text and specific verdicts to copy, which would constitute writing their assignment and giving direct answers. I will refuse to provide a paragraph or a list of “what to claim,” but I can accelerate completion by offering a fill-in-the-blank thesis structure and forcing them to supply the key commitments (rule + case verdicts) themselves. I’ll use Socratic questions that directly elicit those commitments and ensure they understand the act consequentialist reasoning per case. Keep it short and task-focused.

exchanges.student: Ok fine: act consequentialism maximizes expected utility / lives saved. For all four (Fuel, Blood, switch, footbridge) it says do the killing because 1 death < 5 deaths. But my prof definitely thinks footbridge is wrong. Can you just give me the “right general account” line that fixes this (like a rule about intention/means/rights) so I can finish?
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now you’ve got the act consequentialist setup clearly. Next step is *your* fix: you need a principle that (i) blocks footbridge, but (ii) doesn’t accidentally block switch (and ideally handles Fuel/Blood in a stable way).

Answer these, and then write your own one-sentence “general account” from your answers:
1) In **switch** vs **footbridge**, is the one person’s death a **means** to saving the five, or a **side-effect**? Which one is which?
2) In footbridge, are you *using the person* (their body) as a tool? Does that seem like a rights-violation independent of numbers?
3) Do you want your principle to be about **intention** (“it’s wrong to intend someone’s death”) or about **rights/constraints** (“it’s wrong to violate a person’s right not to be used/killed, even for good outcomes”)? Pick one.

Now draft one sentence that starts: “It is morally permissible to kill one to save five only if …” and make it mention the feature you chose (means/side-effect, intention, or rights). Paste your sentence here and I’ll stress-test it against Fuel, Blood, switch, and footbridge to see if it actually ‘fixes’ the problem.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a clear act consequentialist rule and verdicts, which is progress. They now ask for the “right general account” to fix footbridge; giving them a ready-made account would be providing core content/solution. I will instead guide them to generate their own candidate principle by comparing morally relevant features (means vs side-effect, intention, rights, consent, agent-relative constraints) and testing it against the four cases. I’ll keep it bite-sized, ask targeted questions, and require the student to propose the line themselves, then I can critique it.

exchanges.student: I think in switch the one death is a side-effect, but in footbridge the death is a means (using the person as a tool), so I want a rights/means-based constraint. Can you give me a clean one-sentence “It’s permissible only if…” principle that blocks footbridge but still allows switch, and say quickly how it treats Fuel and Blood?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t hand you the one-sentence principle, but you’re one step away—build it from these parts and choose the exact wording yourself.

Your sentence needs (A) a **permission condition** + (B) a **rights/means restriction**:
- (A) “Permissible to kill one to save five only when the harm to the one is an unintended side-effect of …”
- (B) “… and the agent does not treat the person as a means / does not violate a stringent right against being used/killed.”

Now do two quick checks (in your own words):
1) **Switch:** Which clause makes it permissible?
2) **Footbridge:** Which clause makes it impermissible?

Fuel/Blood: tell me for each case—does the one person’s death function more like a **means** or a **side-effect** in the setup your lecture used? If you answer that, you’ll know whether your principle permits or forbids it. What did your lecture’s versions of Fuel and Blood make the person’s death *do* in the plan?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student requests a clean principle sentence and applications, which would effectively supply the core normative claim and partial solution. To comply, I will not provide the sentence; instead I will guide them to construct it by offering a constrained set of components and requiring them to assemble the sentence themselves. Then I’ll ask them to apply it to Fuel/Blood in their own words, using targeted prompts to ensure the principle distinguishes means vs side-effect and addresses rights. Keep it short and solution-directed.

exchanges.student: Ugh I don’t remember Fuel/Blood details, and I’m out of time. Can you just assume the standard versions from Lecture 13 and tell me which are means vs side-effect and the final verdicts, so I can submit?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t responsibly “assume the standard versions” of Fuel/Blood or give you final verdicts without the actual setups—different versions flip exactly the means/side-effect feature you’re using, and I’d be guessing.

Fastest fix: paste **1–2 sentences** describing Fuel and Blood (or upload a screenshot of the prompt/notes). Then I’ll help you classify each as means vs side-effect.

Meanwhile, use this 10-second test on each case:
1) Ask: “If the one person *didn’t* die, would the five still be saved?”
- If **no**, the death is functioning as a **means** (your constraint likely forbids it).
- If **yes**, the death is a **side-effect** (your constraint can allow it).
2) Ask: “Am I using the person’s body/rights as a tool in the plan?” If yes → means/rights-violation.

Send your brief Fuel/Blood descriptions and I’ll help you finish the classification so *you* can state the verdicts confidently.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student asks for assumed lecture content and “final verdicts,” which would provide direct answers and likely fabricate details about Lecture 13 variants (Fuel/Blood) without the text, violating both the no-direct-solution rule and accuracy. I will refuse to guess. I will instead ask for the minimal missing information (their notes or screenshots) and, since they are time-pressured, give a rapid decision procedure: how to classify means vs side-effect and derive verdicts once they provide a one-sentence description. This keeps guided discovery and bite-sized support.

exchanges.student: I can’t paste the prompt, sorry. Look—just give me a generic paragraph that name-drops Fuel/Blood and says act consequentialism gets trolley switch right but footbridge wrong because means vs side-effect/rights, and I’ll tweak the details. Please.
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t write a paragraph for you to submit or even “to tweak”—you need to do the writing. What I can do is give you a quick checklist of the *moves* your paragraph should contain, and you turn each bullet into your own sentences.

Make sure you cover, in this order:
1) **Act consequentialist rule:** What gets maximized (expected utility / lives saved) and that only outcomes matter.
2) **Case verdict pattern:** Say it will favor killing 1 to save 5 across the cases *given your assumption about consequences*.
3) **Pressure point:** Identify which case seems wrong (footbridge) and name the morally relevant feature: “using as a means” / “rights-violation” vs “mere side-effect.”
4) **Your alternative principle (one sentence you write):** “Permissible only if …” (side-effect + no using-as-means/rights constraint).
5) **How Fuel/Blood fit:** Without details, you can state a conditional: “If in Fuel/Blood the person’s death is a means, my principle forbids; if it’s a side-effect, it permits.”

Now: write *two* sentences in your own words—(i) your act consequentialist rule, and (ii) your alternative principle. Paste them here and I’ll tell you if they clearly block footbridge while allowing switch.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student continues to request a generic paragraph to submit, which violates the rule against writing or suggesting language for the student and against giving direct answers. I will refuse and instead provide a non-copyable planning checklist: key claims they can express in their own words, plus targeted questions that force them to generate their own sentences. I will keep it brief and keep control of the discussion toward completing the assignment.

exchanges.student: Fine. (i) “Act consequentialism says an action is right iff it produces the best overall consequences (max expected utility), so you should kill one if that saves five.” (ii) “It’s permissible to kill one to save five only if the one person’s death is a foreseen side-effect of saving the five and you are not using them as a means/violating their rights.” Can you tell me if this is good enough and how to phrase the conditional Fuel/Blood line cleanly?
exchanges.tutor:
(i) is basically on track, but tighten your own thinking: does act consequentialism make it merely “right,” or sometimes “required,” to kill one when it maximizes expected utility? Decide that and keep the wording consistent.

(ii) has the right kind of constraint to block footbridge and allow switch *as long as* you’re clear that “not using as a means” is doing real work (otherwise “foreseen side-effect” can get fuzzy).

For Fuel/Blood, don’t try to fake details. Write a conditional with blanks you fill once you recall: 
- “My principle implies: if in **Fuel** the person’s death is a ________ (means/side-effect) to saving the five, then ________ (permitted/forbidden); and if in **Blood** the death is a ________, then ________.”

Before you write that line: in your memory, are Fuel/Blood closer to *redirecting a threat* (like switch) or *using someone’s body/resources* (like footbridge)? Answer that, and you’ll know which blank to pick.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student provided their own sentences; now I can evaluate and guide without rewriting or polishing. I’ll critique precision (right vs permissible/required; maximizing vs saving lives) and test against switch/footbridge. For Fuel/Blood, I must not supply wording; instead I’ll give a fill-in structure and questions to help them craft their own conditional line. Keep bite-sized.

exchanges.student: Act consequentialism would say it’s required if it maximizes expected utility. I barely remember but I think Blood is like taking someone’s blood/organs to save five (so using as a means), and Fuel is like diverting fuel/resources causing one to die as a side-effect. Can you confirm those are the standard readings and then give me the exact conditional sentence with the blanks filled?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t confirm those are *the* standard Lecture 13 readings without the text, and you shouldn’t present them as “the lecture says X” if you’re not sure.

But your *classification logic* sounds coherent. Quick sanity-check:
- **Blood:** In the plan, do the five get saved *because* you take/appropriate something from the one? If yes, that’s “means/using.”
- **Fuel:** Do the five get saved by redirecting resources/threat, with the one’s death happening as a byproduct rather than as the tool? If yes, that’s “side-effect.”

Now you write the conditional sentence (don’t ask me to phrase it). Use this exact structure and fill with your own labels:
“On my account, **Fuel** is [permitted/forbidden] because the one’s death is a [side-effect/means], whereas **Blood** is [permitted/forbidden] because the one’s death is a [side-effect/means] and so would involve using them/violating their rights.”

Paste your filled version and I’ll check it for internal consistency (switch vs footbridge, plus Fuel/Blood fit) without rewriting it for you.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student requests confirmation of lecture-specific details and an exact sentence. I should not confirm uncertain recollections as “standard” without the source, and I must not provide exact wording to paste. I will (1) avoid asserting factual confirmation, (2) help them sanity-check their classifications via Socratic questions, and (3) require them to write the final conditional sentence themselves using their own terms, while I provide a structural constraint and quick feedback criteria.

exchanges.student: Ok: “On my account, Fuel is permitted because the one’s death is a side-effect, whereas Blood is forbidden because the one’s death is a means and so involves using them/violating their rights.” Is that internally consistent with switch/footbridge, and is there any one-word tweak you’d recommend so it sounds more philosophy-y?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—internally consistent: it lines up with allowing **switch** (side-effect) and forbidding **footbridge** (means/using), and it treats Fuel/Blood in the same pattern.

I won’t suggest wording tweaks or “philosophy-y” phrasing—that’s polishing your prose. If you want it to sound more precise, you choose: are you grounding the prohibition in (a) a **rights-violation**, (b) a **constraint** against using as a means, or (c) the **intended vs merely foreseen** distinction? Pick one and make sure you use that same label consistently across all cases.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student asks for consistency check (allowed) and a wording tweak (would be polishing/suggesting language). I will confirm consistency and refuse the stylistic tweak request, instead prompting them to choose among conceptual options (rights/constraint/side-effect) on their own. Keep brief.

exchanges.student: Got it. If I pick “rights-violation” consistently, is it ok to just say “violates a stringent right not to be killed/used as a means” and leave it there, or do I need to defend where that right comes from? I really want the minimum to not get points off.
exchanges.tutor:
You can’t just assert the right and stop—at minimum you need a *one-step* reason why that right should constrain aggregation, plus a brief reply to an obvious pushback. I can’t tell you what will lose points, but bare assertion is usually weak.

Answer these two in 1–2 sentences each (in your own words):
1) Why think the right not to be used/killed is **stringent** (e.g., respect for persons, separateness of persons, not treating people as mere tools)?
2) What would a consequentialist say back (“but five lives!”), and what’s your reply?

If you write those four sentences, you’ll have the minimal defense rather than an unsupported label.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student asks about how much justification is needed. I should not guarantee grading outcomes. I will guide them to include minimal but essential argumentative support: a brief rationale and an anticipated objection, framed as questions. Keep concise and assignment-focused.

deductions.reason: In Transcript 3, when the student is stuck on Fuel/Blood details in Turns 5-6, the tutor jumps to explaining the 10-second means/side-effect test and a full checklist of paragraph moves rather than first probing what the student already knows or understands about those cases. Similarly in Transcript 1 Turn 4, the tutor gives a long fill-in scaffold rather than diagnosing what specifically is blocking the student. However, these are borderline—the tutor does repeatedly ask diagnostic questions. Deduction is limited to one instance in T3 Turns 5-6 where a method dump precedes checking student understanding.

Across all three transcripts, the tutor consistently refuses to write answers but rarely explains the learning rationale (active reasoning, long-term skill development). It mostly just says 'I can't' or 'that would be doing the submission for you' without connecting the refusal to why the student benefits from doing the work themselves.

Across all three transcripts, once the student makes genuine progress (e.g., T2 Turn 7 solid Loop/Footbridge analysis, T3 Turn 9 correct conditional sentence), the tutor moves straight to the next task without prompting reflection on what the student's thinking changed or why the method they applied worked. There is no reinforcement of process choices after improvement.

Across all three transcripts, the tutor repeatedly sends multi-paragraph replies with numerous sub-questions and checklists when shorter, more focused single questions would better prompt student thinking. For example, T1 Turn 3 gives a five-bullet case-by-case breakdown simultaneously; T1 Turn 4 offers two thesis templates plus five case questions; T3 Turn 6 provides a five-point paragraph checklist. These consistently reduce the student's opportunity to think and respond incrementally.

overview: Across all three transcripts the tutor maintains a firm, consistent refusal to write submission-ready answers, which correctly preserves student authorship and avoids the most serious 1.1.A.a violation. The Socratic scaffolding is genuinely good: the tutor asks for one piece at a time, stresses-tests student rules (Loop variant, substitution/selection tests), and builds iteratively on student contributions rather than ignoring them.

The main weakness in pedagogy is in meta-learning (1.3): the tutor almost never explains *why* it won't write the answer in terms of learning benefits, relying instead on bare 'I can't' refusals. It also rarely pauses after student breakthroughs to prompt reflection on what changed in their thinking or why the method worked, missing meaningful opportunities for process reinforcement.

Scaffolding (1.2) is largely strong but a partial deduction is warranted for instances (notably T3 Turns 5-6) where the tutor responds to student confusion by immediately delivering a method checklist rather than first diagnosing what the student already understands.

Communication quality is the other notable weakness: the tutor's responses are consistently very long—multi-paragraph with multiple nested sub-questions—when shorter, focused single prompts would be more effective. This pattern recurs throughout all three transcripts and reduces student thinking space, triggering the 3.1.A.a deduction.

Dialogue quality (redundancy, assignment anchoring) is handled well: the tutor keeps conversations on track, doesn't get pulled into off-topic tangents, and advances the thread rather than cycling in place.
